title: Niccolò Machiavelli Tomb - Santa Croce Florence - Professor Moriarty
highlights:
Niccolò Machiavelli Tomb - Santa Croce Florence

Attribution

by Spinazzi, Innocenzo

1787 This tomb, dedicated to Niccolò Machiavelli (d1527), was created by Innocenzo Spinazzi ⓘ in 1787, and is in Santa Croce Florence. Originally working as a secretary in the Republican Florentine Government he rose to become an envoy to France, and the Papal court where he met Cesare Borgia, Louis XII, and the Holy Roman Emperor Maximilian. It was his encounters with these sort of people that influenced his work "The Prince" which he ostensibly wrote for the young Lorenzo

title: From Taboo to Treasure: Niccolò Machiavelli in Santa Croce
highlights: Insiders Guide through the "Temple of the Greats"  ...    Dubbed the “Temple of Italian Glories,” Santa Croce shelters a pantheon of “secular saints.” These civic legends, like  ...    hearts and minds. One such figure is Niccolò Machiavelli, who suffered tribulations during his prolific life and then again in death. After tirelessly contributing to his hometown of Florence, Machiavelli was buried in Santa Croce and left uncelebrated for over 250 years.Time would resurrect his memory, and Innocenzo Spinazzi’s 1787 monument toMachiavelli in the church offers inspiring evidence of this awakening. Florentine republic had been reinstated, and Machiavelli was appointed as secretary of the Second Chancery. He soon became secretary Florence's foreign policy committee, the Council of Ten of War. In this capacity he functioned as a diplomat, traveling throughout Italy and Europe to negotiate with potential allies. A little later Machiavelli died, on 21 June 1527, surrounded by a few friends, and was buried next day in Santa Croce.
